mission.title: caddy: proxy: Load Balance using multiple A records per single hostname
mission.description: proxy: Load Balance using multiple A records per single hostname. Requested by 6+ users.

steps.title: Install or upgrade to Caddy v2
steps.description:
Ensure you are running Caddy v2, which includes native reverse proxy load balancing:
```bash
caddy version
```
If running Caddy v1, upgrade to v2 following the official migration guide.

steps.title: Configure reverse_proxy with DNS-based upstreams
steps.description:
In your Caddyfile, configure `reverse_proxy` to point to a hostname that resolves to multiple A records. Caddy will automatically resolve and load balance across all addresses:
```
reverse_proxy backend-service:8080 {
    lb_policy round_robin
}
```
Supported load balancing policies include `round_robin`, `least_conn`, `random`, `first`, `ip_hash`, `uri_hash`, and `header`.

steps.title: Configure dynamic upstreams (optional)
steps.description:
For environments with rapidly changing backends (e.g., Kubernetes), use dynamic upstreams with SRV records or the A record module:
```
reverse_proxy {
    dynamic a backend-service 8080
    lb_policy round_robin
}
```
This re-resolves DNS on each request, ensuring new backends are discovered automatically.

steps.title: Verify load balancing
steps.description:
Test that requests are distributed across all resolved addresses:
```bash
for i in $(seq 1 10); do curl -s http://localhost/health; done
```
Check Caddy logs to confirm requests are being routed to different backend IPs.

resolution.summary: Caddy v2's reverse_proxy directive natively supports DNS-based load balancing. When an upstream hostname resolves to multiple A records, Caddy automatically distributes requests across all resolved addresses. Configure with: `reverse_proxy hostname:port { lb_policy round_robin }`. Caddy v2 also supports dynamic upstreams via SRV records and the `dynamic` upstream module.

prerequisites.description: A working caddy installation or development environment.
